title: Why Material Matters More Than Branding
subtitle: An investigation into what actually determines quality
author: Archive Curator
content:
I used to buy expensive things because they were expensive. Then I started buying expensive things that *lasted* expensive. Different categories entirely.

Here's what I learned: a ₹1000 Supima tee from Uniqlo will outlast a ₹10,000 'luxury' tee because Supima fibres are naturally stronger and softer. The luxury tee has a logo. The Supima tee has evidence.

I now evaluate every purchase by material first, then construction, then everything else. How does the fabric feel? Will it improve with age or deteriorate? Are seams reinforced? Is the hem allowance generous?

This discipline saves money. I own fewer pieces because I buy pieces that last. Common Projects Achilles cost more than the average sneaker, but I'll wear the same pair for a decade. That's not expensive—that's reasonable.

Material is intelligence. Italian leather gets better after five years. Japanese wool doesn't pill after 100 wears. Supima cotton softens with washing. Merino regulates temperature naturally. These aren't marketing claims—I can verify them by wearing.

I stopped trusting brands. I started trusting material.

If something is expensive but made from mediocre material, I skip it regardless of reputation. If something is inexpensive but made from premium material, I investigate further. The archive reflects this: you'll find ₹1000 Uniqlo pieces alongside ₹18,900 Massimo Dutti overcoats, chosen on merit.
